development: Fertilization occurs and the zygote begins to divide rapidly.
tip: Start taking prenatal vitamins with folic acid to support early neural tube development.

development: The fertilized egg implants into the uterine wall and begins forming the placenta.
tip: Continue taking prenatal vitamins and avoid alcohol, smoking, and certain medications.

development: The neural tube begins to form, which will become the brain and spinal cord.
tip: Ensure adequate folic acid intake (600-800 mcg daily) to prevent neural tube defects.

development: The neural tube closes and the heart begins to form and beat.
tip: Schedule your first prenatal appointment and confirm your pregnancy with a healthcare provider.

development: The heart is beating and major organs like the liver and kidneys begin to form.
tip: Eat small, frequent meals to help manage early pregnancy nausea and maintain stable blood sugar.

development: Arms and leg buds appear, and the brain continues developing rapidly.
tip: Stay hydrated and rest when needed - fatigue is common in early pregnancy.

development: Facial features begin to form, and the heart divides into four chambers.
tip: Avoid raw fish, unpasteurized dairy, and deli meats to reduce risk of foodborne illness.

development: Fingers and toes start to form, and the baby begins making small movements.
tip: Listen to your body's signals for rest and don't push yourself too hard.

development: The tail disappears and all essential organs are present, though still developing.
tip: Consider joining a pregnancy support group or connecting with other expecting parents.

development: The baby is now called a fetus, and vital organs are functioning.
tip: Start thinking about your birth plan and discuss options with your healthcare provider.

development: The baby's head makes up about half its body size, and genitals begin to develop.
tip: Continue eating a balanced diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean proteins.

development: Reflexes develop and the baby can open and close fingers and curl toes.
tip: The risk of miscarriage decreases significantly after this week - you can share your news!

development: Fingerprints form and the baby can make facial expressions like squinting.
tip: Your energy may start returning - take advantage to stay active with gentle exercise.

development: The baby can make sucking motions and may begin to suck their thumb.
tip: Start wearing maternity clothes for comfort as your belly begins to show.

development: The baby's legs are now longer than the arms, and fine hair called lanugo covers the body.
tip: Stay hydrated and consider using a pregnancy pillow for better sleep comfort.

development: The baby can hear sounds and may respond to your voice with movement.
tip: You may start feeling baby movements (quickening) - pay attention to these special moments.

development: The skeleton is hardening from cartilage to bone, and fat begins to accumulate.
tip: Practice good posture and consider prenatal yoga or stretching to ease back discomfort.

development: The baby can yawn, hiccup, and swallow, and may be able to hear your heartbeat.
tip: Schedule your anatomy scan (usually between 18-22 weeks) to check baby's development.

development: A protective coating called vernix caseosa begins to form on the baby's skin.
tip: Continue eating iron-rich foods to support increased blood volume and prevent anemia.

development: The baby is halfway through pregnancy and can now hear sounds from outside the womb.
tip: This is a great time to start talking, reading, or singing to your baby - they can hear you!

development: The baby's digestive system is working, and they're swallowing amniotic fluid.
tip: Stay active with low-impact exercises like walking, swimming, or prenatal yoga.

development: The baby's senses are developing - they can feel touch and may respond to light.
tip: Consider starting a pregnancy journal to document your journey and baby's milestones.

development: The baby's skin is still translucent but will become more opaque as fat accumulates.
tip: Practice Kegel exercises to strengthen pelvic floor muscles for labor and recovery.

development: The baby's lungs are developing surfactant, important for breathing after birth.
tip: If you haven't already, start researching childbirth classes and breastfeeding resources.

development: The baby's hands are fully developed and they can make a fist.
tip: Sleep on your side (preferably left side) to improve blood flow to the baby.

development: The baby's eyes can open and close, and they can distinguish between light and dark.
tip: Start preparing your nursery and baby registry - you're entering the third trimester soon!

development: The baby's brain is very active, and they may have regular sleep and wake cycles.
tip: This is the last week of the second trimester - celebrate this milestone!

development: The baby can blink their eyes and has eyelashes. Brain tissue is developing rapidly.
tip: Start counting fetal movements daily - you should feel at least 10 movements in 2 hours.

development: The baby's bones are fully developed but still soft and flexible for birth.
tip: Continue eating small, frequent meals to manage heartburn and maintain energy levels.

development: The baby can regulate their own body temperature and is gaining weight rapidly.
tip: Pack your hospital bag and finalize your birth plan with your healthcare provider.

development: The baby's brain connections are multiplying, and they can process information.
tip: Rest frequently and elevate your feet to reduce swelling in your legs and ankles.

development: The baby's skin is becoming less wrinkled as they gain more fat.
tip: Attend childbirth classes and tour your hospital or birthing center if you haven't already.

development: The baby's immune system is developing, and they're practicing breathing movements.
tip: Stay hydrated and continue taking prenatal vitamins - your baby needs extra nutrients now.

development: The baby's central nervous system is maturing, and their lungs are nearly fully developed.
tip: Practice relaxation techniques and breathing exercises to prepare for labor.

development: The baby's kidneys are fully developed, and their liver can process waste products.
tip: Most babies born at this stage do well, but every week in the womb helps with development.

development: The baby is gaining about half a pound per week and preparing for birth.
tip: Your baby is considered 'early term' now - they could arrive anytime!

development: The baby is considered full-term, and their organs are ready for life outside the womb.
tip: Your baby is full-term! Continue monitoring movements and watch for signs of labor.

development: The baby's brain is still developing rapidly, and they're practicing breathing.
tip: Most babies arrive between 38-42 weeks - be patient and trust your body's timing.

development: The baby's lungs are fully mature and producing surfactant for breathing.
tip: Rest as much as possible - you'll need your energy for labor and caring for your newborn.

development: The baby is ready for birth, and their systems are fully developed.
tip: Your due date is here! Stay calm and remember that only 5% of babies arrive exactly on their due date.

development: The baby continues to gain weight and their skin may be peeling as new skin forms.
tip: Your healthcare provider may discuss induction options if baby hasn't arrived yet - discuss your preferences.

development: The baby is fully developed and ready for birth - post-term pregnancy monitoring is important.
tip: Most providers recommend delivery by 42 weeks - discuss induction or monitoring with your healthcare team.
